title: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis
oneLiner: Hypersensitivity pneumonitis (HP) is an immunologically-mediated lung disease caused by inhaled organic antigens; can mimic infection or interstitial lung disease and cause irreversible fibrosis if missed.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Acute severe asthma / COPD exacerbation** — look for pulsus paradoxus, silent chest, or use of accessory muscles; peak expiratory flow <33% predicted.
- **Pulmonary embolism** — unrehemitting dyspnoea with pleuritic pain, haemoptysis, or syncope; use Wells’ score and D-dimer if possible, but CT pulmonary angiography if high suspicion.
- **Bilateral pneumonia (e.g. viral/bacterial)** — high fever (>38.5°C), productive cough, focal crackles/consolidation on exam; CRP >100 mg/L or procalcitonin >0.5 ng/mL favour infection over HP.
sections.Presentation:
- **Acute HP:** fever, chills, flu-like symptoms 4–8 hours after antigen exposure; dry cough, dyspnoea, bilateral inspiratory crackles.
- **Subacute/Chronic HP:** insidious dyspnoea, cough, weight loss, fatigue; fine basilar crackles, clubbing (late), no finger clubbing in acute.
- **Key details to elicit:** history of exposure (birds, mouldy hay, hot tubs, air-conditioning, farming, bird-keeping).
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Infective pneumonia** — high fever, lobar consolidation on CXR, productive cough, rapid response to antibiotics.
- **Asthma/COPD exacerbation** – wheeze, hyperinflation, airflow limitation (PEFR/FEV1), atopy.
- **Acute interstitial pneumonia / ARDS** – very rapid onset, bilateral opacities, severe hypoxia; no clear exposure history.
- **Sarcoidosis** – bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, erythema nodosum, raised ACE; non-caseating granulomas on biopsy.
- **Splitter:** presence of systemic symptoms (fever, myalgia) AND a clear (recent) antigen exposure within hours → HP, not infection.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** pulse oximetry (aim SpO2 >94%), respiratory rate, peak flow; check for tachypnoea and hypoxia at rest/exertion.
- **Bloods:** FBC (neutrophilia/infection), CRP/ESR (often moderately raised), LFTs (sometimes raised), and (if acute) arterial blood gas (ABG) with lactate.
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line: CXR** – may be normal or show bilateral reticulonodular opacities, ground-glass.
  - **Second-line: HRCT chest (high-resolution CT)** – classic: centrilobular ground-glass nodules, mosaic attenuation, air trapping on expiratory cuts; chronic HP shows reticulation, traction bronchiectasis, honeycombing.
- **Special tests:**
  - **Serum precipitins (IgG against specific antigens)** – not always positive; false negatives common.
  - **Bronchoalveolar lavage** – lymphocytosis >50% (especially CD4+/CD8+ ratio inverted).
  - **Lung biopsy (if diagnosis unclear)** – granulomas (non-caseating), cellular bronchiolitis.
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:** HP diagnostic criteria (from ATS guidelines) – presence of compatible symptoms, exposure history, HRCT pattern, BAL lymphocytosis, + serum antibodies (if available); no single validated score routinely used.
- **Never-miss test:** **HRCT chest** – without it, you may treat for pneumonia or miss chronic fibrotic HP.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):** oxygen to target SpO2 ≥94%; if severe hypoxia (FiO2 >40% to maintain SpO2), escalate to senior/ICU; stop antigen exposure (remove patient from source).
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - If acute and moderate–severe: **oral prednisolone** 0.5–1 mg/kg/day (max 60 mg) for 2–4 weeks, then taper over 4–8 weeks – [check local policy for tapering schedule].
  - If subacute/chronic: prednisolone as above only if rapidly progressive or severe; consider steroid-sparing agents (azathioprine, mycophenolate) under specialist.
  - **Supportive:** oxygen, physiotherapy, nutritional support if weight loss.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Complete antigen avoidance** – this is the cornerstone; refer to occupational health / housing if needed.
  - Follow-up respiratory clinic – often monthly initially; repeat PFTs (spirometry, DLCO).
  - Consider lung transplantation for end-stage fibrotic HP.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** IV fluids – 0.9% sodium chloride 1L over 8h if oral intake poor; VTE prophylaxis – dalteparin 5000 units SC daily (if eGFR >30, [check local policy]); NBM only if required for bronchoscopy; observations – NEWS2 4–6 hourly.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line: Prednisolone** – oral, 30–60 mg daily (0.5–1 mg/kg) for 2–4 weeks, then taper by 5–10 mg per week as guided – [check local policy for exact taper].
- **Alternatives:** intravenous methylprednisolone (1 g daily for 3 days) in acute severe HP or if oral failure; then oral prednisolone.
- **Key contraindications:** active infection (must treat first); caution in osteoporosis, diabetes, hypertension – monitor glucose, BP.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** prednisolone – no dose adjustment in mild-to-moderate impairment; in severe hepatic impairment, consider reduced dose (e.g. 50%).
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Abrupt withdrawal of high-dose prednisolone (>40 mg daily for >3 weeks)** – risk of adrenal crisis; prescribe a written tapering plan (e.g. "reduce by 5 mg weekly").
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:** acute respiratory failure, superimposed infection (especially *P. jirovecii* pneumonia if immunosuppressed), barotrauma from ventilatory support.
- **Chronic:** irreversible pulmonary fibrosis, pulmonary hypertension, cor pulmonale, reduced exercise tolerance, death.
sections.Prognosis:
- **Acute HP:** rapid improvement with antigen avoidance alone (within days). Good prognosis if treated early.
- **Subacute/Chronic HP:** ~50% develop fibrosis despite treatment; 5-year mortality ~30–60% in fibrotic cases. Lung function may stabilise but rarely normalises.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always ask about hobbies and occupation** – bird-keeping, farming, sauna/hot tub use, mould in home, air-conditioning filters.
- **Order HRCT with expiratory views** – air trapping on expiration is classic and helps differentiate from other ILDs.
- **Arrange lung function** (spirometry, DLCO, lung volumes) before and after steroids to assess reversibility.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Treating as pneumonia with broad-spectrum antibiotics** – this delays antigen avoidance and steroids; always think HP in patients with recurrent "pneumonia" after specific exposures.
- **Giving intravenous methylprednisolone without first checking for active infection** – can precipitate sepsis; do blood cultures, CRP, and CXR first.
- **Sending home without clear follow-up** – HP can rapidly progress; arrange respiratory clinic review within 4 weeks.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Worsening breathlessness (e.g. SpO2 <92% at rest or exertion on room air), fever, productive cough, or inability to walk without oxygen.
- **Follow-up:** Respiratory clinic in 4–6 weeks; repeat CXR or HRCT as directed; monitor symptoms and PFTs.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior/ICU** if:
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or rising despite oxygen.
  - SpO2 <90% on FiO2 >40%.
  - Respiratory acidosis (pH <7.35, pCO2 >6.5 kPa) or lactate >2 mmol/L on ABG.
  - Altered consciousness, respiratory rate >30 breaths/min, or haemodynamic instability (SBP <90 mmHg).
  - Suspected endotracheal intubation needed.

rawMarkdown:
## Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis
**One-liner:** Hypersensitivity pneumonitis (HP) is an immunologically-mediated lung disease caused by inhaled organic antigens; can mimic infection or interstitial lung disease and cause irreversible fibrosis if missed.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Acute severe asthma / COPD exacerbation** — look for pulsus paradoxus, silent chest, or use of accessory muscles; peak expiratory flow <33% predicted.
- **Pulmonary embolism** — unrehemitting dyspnoea with pleuritic pain, haemoptysis, or syncope; use Wells’ score and D-dimer if possible, but CT pulmonary angiography if high suspicion.
- **Bilateral pneumonia (e.g. viral/bacterial)** — high fever (>38.5°C), productive cough, focal crackles/consolidation on exam; CRP >100 mg/L or procalcitonin >0.5 ng/mL favour infection over HP.

### Presentation
- **Acute HP:** fever, chills, flu-like symptoms 4–8 hours after antigen exposure; dry cough, dyspnoea, bilateral inspiratory crackles.
- **Subacute/Chronic HP:** insidious dyspnoea, cough, weight loss, fatigue; fine basilar crackles, clubbing (late), no finger clubbing in acute.
- **Key details to elicit:** history of exposure (birds, mouldy hay, hot tubs, air-conditioning, farming, bird-keeping).

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Infective pneumonia** — high fever, lobar consolidation on CXR, productive cough, rapid response to antibiotics.
- **Asthma/COPD exacerbation** – wheeze, hyperinflation, airflow limitation (PEFR/FEV1), atopy.
- **Acute interstitial pneumonia / ARDS** – very rapid onset, bilateral opacities, severe hypoxia; no clear exposure history.
- **Sarcoidosis** – bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, erythema nodosum, raised ACE; non-caseating granulomas on biopsy.
- **Splitter:** presence of systemic symptoms (fever, myalgia) AND a clear (recent) antigen exposure within hours → HP, not infection.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** pulse oximetry (aim SpO2 >94%), respiratory rate, peak flow; check for tachypnoea and hypoxia at rest/exertion.
- **Bloods:** FBC (neutrophilia/infection), CRP/ESR (often moderately raised), LFTs (sometimes raised), and (if acute) arterial blood gas (ABG) with lactate.
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line: CXR** – may be normal or show bilateral reticulonodular opacities, ground-glass.
  - **Second-line: HRCT chest (high-resolution CT)** – classic: centrilobular ground-glass nodules, mosaic attenuation, air trapping on expiratory cuts; chronic HP shows reticulation, traction bronchiectasis, honeycombing.
- **Special tests:**
  - **Serum precipitins (IgG against specific antigens)** – not always positive; false negatives common.
  - **Bronchoalveolar lavage** – lymphocytosis >50% (especially CD4+/CD8+ ratio inverted).
  - **Lung biopsy (if diagnosis unclear)** – granulomas (non-caseating), cellular bronchiolitis.
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:** HP diagnostic criteria (from ATS guidelines) – presence of compatible symptoms, exposure history, HRCT pattern, BAL lymphocytosis, + serum antibodies (if available); no single validated score routinely used.
- **Never-miss test:** **HRCT chest** – without it, you may treat for pneumonia or miss chronic fibrotic HP.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):** oxygen to target SpO2 ≥94%; if severe hypoxia (FiO2 >40% to maintain SpO2), escalate to senior/ICU; stop antigen exposure (remove patient from source).
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - If acute and moderate–severe: **oral prednisolone** 0.5–1 mg/kg/day (max 60 mg) for 2–4 weeks, then taper over 4–8 weeks – [check local policy for tapering schedule].
  - If subacute/chronic: prednisolone as above only if rapidly progressive or severe; consider steroid-sparing agents (azathioprine, mycophenolate) under specialist.
  - **Supportive:** oxygen, physiotherapy, nutritional support if weight loss.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Complete antigen avoidance** – this is the cornerstone; refer to occupational health / housing if needed.
  - Follow-up respiratory clinic – often monthly initially; repeat PFTs (spirometry, DLCO).
  - Consider lung transplantation for end-stage fibrotic HP.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** IV fluids – 0.9% sodium chloride 1L over 8h if oral intake poor; VTE prophylaxis – dalteparin 5000 units SC daily (if eGFR >30, [check local policy]); NBM only if required for bronchoscopy; observations – NEWS2 4–6 hourly.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line: Prednisolone** – oral, 30–60 mg daily (0.5–1 mg/kg) for 2–4 weeks, then taper by 5–10 mg per week as guided – [check local policy for exact taper].
- **Alternatives:** intravenous methylprednisolone (1 g daily for 3 days) in acute severe HP or if oral failure; then oral prednisolone.
- **Key contraindications:** active infection (must treat first); caution in osteoporosis, diabetes, hypertension – monitor glucose, BP.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** prednisolone – no dose adjustment in mild-to-moderate impairment; in severe hepatic impairment, consider reduced dose (e.g. 50%).
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Abrupt withdrawal of high-dose prednisolone (>40 mg daily for >3 weeks)** – risk of adrenal crisis; prescribe a written tapering plan (e.g. "reduce by 5 mg weekly").

### Complications
- **Acute:** acute respiratory failure, superimposed infection (especially *P. jirovecii* pneumonia if immunosuppressed), barotrauma from ventilatory support.
- **Chronic:** irreversible pulmonary fibrosis, pulmonary hypertension, cor pulmonale, reduced exercise tolerance, death.

### Prognosis
- **Acute HP:** rapid improvement with antigen avoidance alone (within days). Good prognosis if treated early.
- **Subacute/Chronic HP:** ~50% develop fibrosis despite treatment; 5-year mortality ~30–60% in fibrotic cases. Lung function may stabilise but rarely normalises.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always ask about hobbies and occupation** – bird-keeping, farming, sauna/hot tub use, mould in home, air-conditioning filters.
- **Order HRCT with expiratory views** – air trapping on expiration is classic and helps differentiate from other ILDs.
- **Arrange lung function** (spirometry, DLCO, lung volumes) before and after steroids to assess reversibility.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Treating as pneumonia with broad-spectrum antibiotics** – this delays antigen avoidance and steroids; always think HP in patients with recurrent "pneumonia" after specific exposures.
- **Giving intravenous methylprednisolone without first checking for active infection** – can precipitate sepsis; do blood cultures, CRP, and CXR first.
- **Sending home without clear follow-up** – HP can rapidly progress; arrange respiratory clinic review within 4 weeks.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Worsening breathlessness (e.g. SpO2 <92% at rest or exertion on room air), fever, productive cough, or inability to walk without oxygen.
- **Follow-up:** Respiratory clinic in 4–6 weeks; repeat CXR or HRCT as directed; monitor symptoms and PFTs.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior/ICU** if:
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or rising despite oxygen.
  - SpO2 <90% on FiO2 >40%.
  - Respiratory acidosis (pH <7.35, pCO2 >6.5 kPa) or lactate >2 mmol/L on ABG.
  - Altered consciousness, respiratory rate >30 breaths/min, or haemodynamic instability (SBP <90 mmHg).
  - Suspected endotracheal intubation needed.
